item.title: Hygiene firm score historic hat trick
item.description: SOME achievements define a company. Others make history. For FLiCK Hygiene, it was yet another milestone to become the first pest management company in Fiji to win an industry award for the third time.Last week, FLiCK Hygiene, a fully locally owned pest control hygiene service provider, yet again won the AEPMA South Pacific Pest Manager of the Year Award at the FAOPMA Pest Summit 2026 in Auckland,
item.content:encoded:
SOME achievements define a company. Others make history. For FLiCK Hygiene, it was yet another milestone to become the first pest management company in Fiji to win an industry award for the third time.Last week, FLiCK Hygiene, a fully locally owned pest control hygiene service provider, yet again won the AEPMA South Pacific Pest Manager of the Year Award at the FAOPMA Pest Summit 2026 in Auckland, New Zealand – for three consecutive years: 2024, 2025, and 2026.

The Australian Environmental Pest Managers Association (AEPMA) represents professional pest managers across Australia, sets standards for the pest management industry and provides information to consumers on urban pest control.

“We are incredibly proud to celebrate an extraordinary milestone as FLiCK Hygiene becomes the first pest management company in Fiji to win the AEPMA South Pacific Pest Manager of the Year Award for three consecutive years—2024, 2025, and 2026,” FLiCK Hygience said in a statement.

“That’s a remarkable hat-trick of excellence, and one that reflects our unwavering commitment to raising the standard of professional pest management throughout Fiji and the South Pacific.

“This award is far more than a trophy—it is recognition of the passion, professionalism, innovation and dedication that our people bring to work every single day. “It also adds to the long list of ‘firsts’ that FLiCK Hygiene has achieved over the years as we continue to lead the industry and set new benchmarks for excellence.”

Upon receiving the award, the company acknowledged its staff and clients.

“From our technicians working tirelessly in the field, to our supervisors, managers, administration team, and everyone supporting our operations behind the scenes—thank you. Your commitment to quality, safety, customer service, and continuous improvement has made this historic hat-trick possible. We couldn’t be prouder of what we have accomplished together.

“To our valued customers and business partners, thank you for your continued trust and confidence in FLiCK Hygiene. Your support has been instrumental in our journey, and we remain committed to providing the exceptional service and innovative pest management solutions you have come to expect from us.

“As we celebrate this milestone, we also look ahead. We will continue to invest in our people, embrace innovation, and strive for even greater achievements as we work to protect homes, businesses, and communities across Fiji.

“Three consecutive titles. One incredible team. One historic hat-trick. Countless reasons to be proud.”

AEPMA’s Pest Management of the Year Awards is an annual event to promote the professionalism of the pest management industry to the general public and to promote the wider pest management industry.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: BUSINESS OPINION I An industry that milked the farmers
item.description: A 500-GRAM block of Rewa Butter now costs close to $16. A Fijian on the national minimum wage of $5 an hour must work three hours to buy it — before tax, before bus fare, before anything else on the table. The brand is a century old and unmistakably Fijian. What sits behind it is not. For a hundred years the story told about Rewa was simple: Fijian cows, Fijian hands, Fijian families.
item.content:encoded:
A 500-GRAM block of Rewa Butter now costs close to $16. A Fijian on the national minimum wage of $5 an hour must work three hours to buy it — before tax, before bus fare, before anything else on the table.

The brand is a century old and unmistakably Fijian. What sits behind it is not. For a hundred years the story told about Rewa was simple: Fijian cows, Fijian hands, Fijian families.

The real story is slower and darker — an industry that has been milking its own farmers for decades, first through mismanagement, then by decree, and now through a market structure that keeps them supplying the milk while a private conglomerate keeps the money.A cooperative born in 1924

After the First World War, returned servicemen were settled on land donated by Fijian chiefs under the Tailevu Dairy Scheme.

In 1924 they built Fiji’s first butter factory at Korovou, still called the industry’s birthplace. It grew into the Rewa Co-operative Dairy Company, farmer-owned in name, supplied at its 1991 peak by 2041 registered farms. For most of the century, Rewa Butter really was churned from Fijian milk. That era did not fade through market forces.

It was ended, deliberately, through internal failure so severe that by 2012, the government dismantled the structure that had carried the Rewa name for eighty-eight years.

In 2003, board members raised allegations serious enough that Rewa Dairy commissioned PricewaterhouseCoopers to audit its own accounts. The 2004 report found escalating “loans” to directors, farmers and shareholders — $624,189 in 2006, rising to $868,957 by 2009 — including a $5700 Omega watch billed to the company address.

The findings went to the Director of Public Prosecutions. Nothing happened for eight years. “Rewa Dairy has been riddled with mismanagement for decades,” Attorney-General Aiyaz Sayed-Khaiyum told the nation in 2012.

“When the accounts were audited, there was a $5 million hole that nobody could explain.” Even the rescue could not agree on its own numbers: Southern Cross Foods’ debt take-on has been cited at both $7 million and $17 million in separate Parliamentary accounts.

The 2012 decree split the company in two. Fiji Dairy Limited became “the sole processor of milk in Fiji.”

The Fiji Cooperative Dairy Company, owned by farmer-stakeholders, kept the cows and the collecting — nothing else.

It has no processing capacity of its own, then or now; every litre is sold upward to a factory farmers do not control.

Southern Cross Foods, a CJ Patel subsidiary, took 80 percent of Fiji Dairy Limited; farmers held the rest. Today Fiji Dairy draws from more than 350 farmers to run one Nabua factory employing roughly 200 people — the growing side of the industry outnumbers the earning side and owns none of it.

None of this is accidental. In Parliament in 2017, then-Opposition MP Biman Prasad noted Fiji Dairy Ltd “continues to be a healthy and profitable entity” while competing milk importers pay a 32 percent duty (15 percent on ghee) — a rate Fiji Dairy alone was exempted from for ten years under the original 2012 deal.

One commercial business database estimates Fiji Dairy’s 2026 annual revenue at around $5 million.

Set against that: the entire farmer-shareholder base has never collectively received more than $1.2 million in dividends in any year on record — and in some years, nothing at all.

CJ Patel Group, Southern Cross Foods’ parent, controls more than twenty companies, its distribution arm carrying Nivea, Colgate-Palmolive, Kimberly-Clark, GSK’s Panadol and Nestlé’s Fiji range into the same supermarkets where Rewa Butter sits. No regulator has ever examined the whole picture at once.

Here is what the wrapper does not say. Rewa-branded milk powder sold today is labelled “Product of New Zealand. Packed in Fiji-by-Fiji Dairy Pte Ltd.”

Fiji tried to manufacture its own powder once. In 1999, Rewa Dairy spent over a million dollars on evaporator equipment with no feasibility study, then discovered it needed roughly a hundred litres of raw milk, and enough burnt energy to cost $25, to produce one kilogram of powder. They sold the heater for $130,000.

“The evaporator is still sitting at Fiji Dairy,” Sayed-Khaiyum told Parliament in 2012 — a monument to the moment Fiji quietly gave up manufacturing for good.

Even before 2012, under farmer ownership, Rewa Dairy’s own profits came mainly from imported powder, butter and cheese, not from the milk its members produced.

Rewa Butter’s 500-gram pack moves like the commodity it is. It sat at $10.60 in early 2024, rose to $12.73 by June, $13.94 by July, and $15.93 by mid-2025 — called a “luxury item” in Parliament.

A global butter glut briefly cut it to $14.28 in December 2025, before landing costs pushed it back toward $16 this year.

Every movement traces to the cost of imported bulk butter, set in Auckland and Melbourne, not Nausori. The commission does not negotiate that price down; it approves the mark-up once the paperwork proves it real.

While the shelf price climbs, the farm gate tells the opposite story and has for over a decade. FCDCL’s own chief executive told Parliament the price of raw milk fell from $1 a litre in 2012, the year of the restructuring, to 80 cents by 2017.

In the chaotic months right after privatisation, it swung as low as 52 cents some weeks. It has since recovered to today’s $1.07 to $1.12 — still barely above where it started fourteen years ago. Ravindra Prasad of Nandan’s Dairy Farm in Viria, Naitasiri, now produces 150 to 160 litres a day, down from 200; his weekly feed bill has doubled to $300.

Harvindar Singh of J Singh Dairy Farm in Waimaro has watched daily output fall from 1600 to 1300 litres, while a bag of copra feed once costing $8 is now nearly $30, and his monthly fuel bill has doubled to $10,000. Both are asking for $1.50 to $1.80 — an ask that would barely restore, in real terms, what they were promised the day the industry was sold.

FCDCL has now confirmed that farm-gate pricing will be set independently by the FCCC, taking the one lever farmers still touched out of their hands.

None of this is new. Fiji’s agriculture policy has chased “food self-sufficiency” since the 1970s and, by government’s own admission, never reached it. In the narrow fresh-milk market, local supply covers roughly half to two-thirds of demand. Once butter, powder and cheese — almost entirely imported — are counted into the whole dairy diet, local farmers supply barely a quarter.

Production concentrates overwhelmingly in the Central Division; the Western Division supplies most of the rest, while the Northern and Eastern Divisions barely register.

item.title: FCCC cuts interim sugar harvesting, cartage rates after fuel price drop
item.description: The Fijian Competition and Consumer Commission has reduced interim mechanical harvesting and sugarcane cartage rates following a decline in fuel prices, saying the changes are intended to better reflect current operating costs. Mechanical harvesting rates have been reduced by 16.7 per cent, from $24.63 to $20.52 per tonne, while interim lorry cartage rates between Rakiraki and the Rarawai Sugar Mi
item.content:encoded:
The Fijian Competition and Consumer Commission has reduced interim mechanical harvesting and sugarcane cartage rates following a decline in fuel prices, saying the changes are intended to better reflect current operating costs.

Mechanical harvesting rates have been reduced by 16.7 per cent, from $24.63 to $20.52 per tonne, while interim lorry cartage rates between Rakiraki and the Rarawai Sugar Mill have been cut by 14.5 per cent.

The FCCC said the cartage adjustment applies only to the Rakiraki-Rarawai route, which remains subject to price regulation under the Control of Prices for Sugar Cane Cartage Lorry Services from Rakiraki to Rarawai Order 2022.

FCCC Chief Executive Officer Senikavika Jiuta said the reductions were largely driven by falling fuel prices.

“As fuel costs have decreased, it is appropriate that the resulting savings are reflected in the regulated rates to ensure they remain aligned with actual operating costs.”

Ms Jiuta said the revised rates demonstrate the Commission’s commitment to maintaining fair, evidence-based and responsive pricing.

“This adjustment reflects FCCC’s commitment to maintaining fair, evidence-based and responsive pricing that protects the interests of growers while allowing service providers to recover reasonable costs.”

The Commission said it will continue monitoring global fuel prices, industry conditions and other operating costs and will make further regulatory adjustments where necessary.

Farmers and consumers have also been urged to report any instances of overcharging or unfair trading practices by mechanical harvester operators or sugarcane cartage providers to the FCCC.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Police officer found dead in Sigatoka
item.description: The Fiji Police Force has confirmed that the body of a man found in Sigatoka this morning is that of a serving police officer. In a statement, police said the circumstances surrounding the officer’s death are now under investigation.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Police Force has confirmed that the body of a man found in Sigatoka this morning is that of a serving police officer.

In a statement, police said the circumstances surrounding the officer’s death are now under investigation.

A post-mortem examination will be conducted to determine the cause of death, with further details expected to be released once the examination is completed.

The Police has not said if the officer was on duty or not.

Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu has extended the Fiji Police Force’s condolences to the officer’s family during what he described as a difficult time.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Children’s rights misunderstood, says Khan
item.description: Some children misunderstand their rights and use the concept as a reason to avoid responsibilities, says Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission commissioner Chantelle Khan. Ms Khan said there was often confusion around children’s rights, particularly when discussing issues such as corporal punishment and discipline.
item.content:encoded:
Some children misunderstand their rights and use the concept as a reason to avoid responsibilities, says Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission commissioner Chantelle Khan.

Ms Khan said there was often confusion around children’s rights, particularly when discussing issues such as corporal punishment and discipline.

“Take children’s rights and corporal punishment as an example. There is often resistance because some people believe children’s rights simply mean children can disobey their parents,” she said.

“That is not what children’s rights are about.”

She said children’s rights focused on ensuring children had access to education, a safe home and protection from violence.

“It is about understanding the depth of those rights.”

Ms Khan said the commission continued to carry out education and awareness programs because Fiji still had progress to make in improving understanding of human rights.

“Apart from handling complaints, we also conduct education and awareness programs because Fiji still has a long way to go in understanding human rights.”

She said the commission worked with schools, church groups and communities to promote awareness.

“Partnerships with traditional leaders were also important in reaching communities.”

Ms Khan said working alongside traditional institutions would help strengthen human rights education across communities.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: BACK TRACKS | Meke – more than a performance
item.description: The Fijian meke is more than just a dance. It is the voice of a people, the keeper of stories and a bridge between generations.
item.content:encoded:
The Fijian meke is more than just a dance.

It is the voice of a people, the keeper of stories and a bridge between generations.

Speaking as a music teacher, Mesake Vuniwai says it is a responsibility.

Every week, the 43-year-old from Vaturua in the district of Nakelo stands before young men and women at the National Youth Training Centre in Valelevu, teaching them not only the movements of the meke, but also the chants, discipline and traditional protocols that give each performance its meaning.

“I always tell my students that our traditional meke is the only way we can tell the story about us,” he said.

“If we don’t teach it the right way, the children coming after us will think the modern way is the real meke.”

Vuniwai said it was a responsibility he did not take lightly. Although his studies at the Fiji Institute of Technology gave him the opportunity to pursue music professionally, Vuniwai believes it was not his education that took him around the world.

It was the cultural knowledge passed down by his elders.

From Taiwan and Dubai to Japan, China, Australia, New Zealand, Hawaii and American Samoa, he has proudly represented Fiji through traditional meke.

“Every performance reinforced one belief — that Fiji’s greatest gift to the world is its authentic culture.

“When we perform our culture in a 100 per cent traditional way, it talks about us.”

Looking back, Vuniwai said his journey began long before he travelled overseas or performed before international audiences.

He said while other children played outside, he was content sitting quietly beside the tanoa during family and village gatherings, listening to elders speak and watching every step of traditional protocol unfold before him.

“The love of our tradition inspired me the most.

“Since I was little, I would sit beside the tanoa and watch the traditional protocol step by step. When I grew up, I wanted to be like that.”

Those moments became the foundation of everything that followed.

Vuniwai believes the greatest lessons in life are not always learned in schools.

Instead, they are passed from one generation to the next by elders willing to share their knowledge.

“The best way to learn is to sit down during family or village gatherings and just listen.

“Listen to how the elders speak. Listen to the traditional protocol.”

He said many young people today were missing those opportunities.

“Before, everyone sat quietly and listened.

“The knowledge will always come from the elders. If you want to learn your culture, be part of those gatherings.”

His passion eventually led him to the Fiji Institute of Technology in 2006, where he trained under respected cultural practitioner master Levi Koso.

There, he learned not only music but also the discipline and cultural protocols that accompany authentic meke.

That same year, he travelled to Taiwan with the FIT Meke Group for the Austronesian Festival of Arts before representing Fiji again in Dubai in 2007.

After graduating, Vuniwai said he joined the Conservatorium of Music, where opportunities to perform overseas continued.

Together with fellow performers, he travelled throughout the Pacific, Oceania and Asia, sharing Fiji’s traditions with audiences from around the world.

He later became a member of Dolce Sounds Dulali, a traditional performing group formed by former students of master Levi Koso to preserve authentic meke, traditional chants and old songs.

The group also played an important role in promoting Fiji’s tourism industry through performances across Australia, New Zealand and the United States.

item.title: Schools cannot fight challenges alone – Bure
item.description: Fiji’s schools cannot overcome growing challenges such as student absenteeism, drug and substance abuse, ageing infrastructure and teacher shortages without the collective support of communities, parents and education stakeholders, says the Ministry of Education. Speaking on behalf of Education Minister Aseri Radrodro at the School Management Association of Fiji’s 11th National conference in Suva
item.content:encoded:
Fiji’s schools cannot overcome growing challenges such as student absenteeism, drug and substance abuse, ageing infrastructure and teacher shortages without the collective support of communities, parents and education stakeholders, says the Ministry of Education.

Speaking on behalf of Education Minister Aseri Radrodro at the School Management Association of Fiji’s 11th National conference in Suva on Monday, deputy secretary Timoci Bure said education today went far beyond teaching and learning.

“Schools are responding to issues that affect the lives of our students every day,” Mr Bure said.

“Student attendance, drugs and substance abuse, ageing infrastructure, teacher shortages and student wellbeing continue to influence the learning environment in many of our schools.

“These are challenges that no school can address alone, and they require practical solutions, informed leadership and the support of everyone with a role in education.”

Mr Bure said despite the challenges, he remained encouraged by the commitment shown by educators and communities across the country.

“What gives me confidence is what I continue to see across Fiji.

“I have met heads of schools who refuse to give up on students, who are at risk of dropping out.

“I have seen teachers giving additional time to learners who need extra support.

“I have watched communities come together to improve school facilities, not because they were asked to, but because they believed it was the right thing to do.”

He said the conference provided an opportunity for school leaders and management committees to build on existing progress by sharing practical experiences and developing new strategies.

“Now is the time to build on that progress. It is an opportunity to reflect on the progress already made, exchange practical experiences and identify new ways to strengthen the support provided to our schools.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Singh: Reward school managers
item.description: School managers, who volunteer their time and resources to keep Fiji’s schools running, deserve greater recognition and financial support, says School Management Association of Fiji president Govind Singh. Speaking at the opening of the association’s three-day 11th National conference in Suva on Monday, Mr Singh said many school managers had no office and received no compensation despite being on
item.content:encoded:
School managers, who volunteer their time and resources to keep Fiji’s schools running, deserve greater recognition and financial support, says School Management Association of Fiji president Govind Singh.

Speaking at the opening of the association’s three-day 11th National conference in Suva on Monday, Mr Singh said many school managers had no office and received no compensation despite being on call to assist schools.

“If you do a survey, you will find hardly any office for the manager,” he said.

“You can imagine the manager is a farmer. He goes into the interior to tend to his plantation, and then all of a sudden, a call comes from the principal’s office requesting them for their assistance in the school.

“The poor guy leaves his stock and everything there, and runs to the school.”

Mr Singh said managers often made personal sacrifices without any financial assistance.

“He gets no compensation, but for how long? The managers are used like a pari, taxi or something like that.”

He called on the Government to reinstate honorariums for school managers, saying these were removed under the 2014 education grant formula.

“Before 2014, managers used to get honorarium… not a single cent of grant money should be used by the management.”

Mr Singh questioned why school grants could fund principals and head teachers to attend conferences while managers could not receive travel assistance for their own conference.

“What a shame! It appears as if we have lost our culture about how we treat people who have done so much in this country.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
